coredata.dc:title: Aggregation-induced emission solid-state multicolor fluorescent carbon dots for LEDs and fingerprints applications
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jiyao
Zhang, Yuman
Guo, Xiangjun
Zhang, Hongmei
Deng, Yujia
Zhao, Xihui
coredata.dc:description: At present, most of the reported carbon dots (CDs) are still dispersed in the solvent in liquid state. However, solid-state CDs cannot fundamentally prevent the aggregation-caused quenching effect (ACQ), which seriously affects their application. Herein, hydrophobic solid-state luminescent CDs with typical aggregation-induced emission (AIE) behavior were synthesized by simple solvothermal method using dithiosalicylic acid (DSTA) and acetic acid. The AIE behavior of CDs is mainly due to the presence of disulfide bonds, which restrict the intramolecular rotation of CDs surface groups around them, thus generating solid-state fluorescence (SSF) in long-wave emission. In addition, by using MA, urea and sulfonamide to tune the emission behavior of CDs, red, yellow and green AIE CDs named R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs were obtained successfully. The as-prepared CDs powder was further applied to fabricate multi-color light-emitting diodes (LEDs) on UV chips. The fabricated tri-color LED devices can maintain high color purity and color rendering index (CRI). More importantly, the Correlated color temperature (CCT) of the three LED devices was located in the warm light area, which could effectively reduce visual fatigue. Finally, we applied three kinds of AIE CDs in fingerprint detection and effectively displayed the details.

head.ce:title: Aggregation-induced emission solid-state multicolor fluorescent carbon dots for LEDs and fingerprints applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: At present, most of the reported carbon dots (CDs) are still dispersed in the solvent in liquid state. However, solid-state CDs cannot fundamentally prevent the aggregation-caused quenching effect (ACQ), which seriously affects their application. Herein, hydrophobic solid-state luminescent CDs with typical aggregation-induced emission (AIE) behavior were synthesized by simple solvothermal method using dithiosalicylic acid (DSTA) and acetic acid. The AIE behavior of CDs is mainly due to the presence of disulfide bonds, which restrict the intramolecular rotation of CDs surface groups around them, thus generating solid-state fluorescence (SSF) in long-wave emission. In addition, by using MA, urea and sulfonamide to tune the emission behavior of CDs, red, yellow and green AIE CDs named R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs were obtained successfully. The as-prepared CDs powder was further applied to fabricate multi-color light-emitting diodes (LEDs) on UV chips. The fabricated tri-color LED devices can maintain high color purity and color rendering index (CRI). More importantly, the Correlated color temperature (CCT) of the three LED devices was located in the warm light area, which could effectively reduce visual fatigue. Finally, we applied three kinds of AIE CDs in fingerprint detection and effectively displayed the details.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of 0-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, which have a wide range of applications in biosensing [1], photocatalysis [2], detection [3], bioimaging [4], and cancer therapy [5]. The easy preparation and purification, good biocompatibility, and tunable structural and optical properties of CDs make it focused [6–8]. At present, most of the reported CDs have good water solubility, and their applications are mostly concentrated in aqueous solutions [9,10]. Unfortunately, these CDs suffer from aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) during drying process due to excessive fluorescence resonance energy transfer or stacking of π-π bond [11,12]. This fluorescence aggregation quenching hinders the further development and application of CDs. To solve the above problems, most researchers choose to combine CDs with solid substrates (such as PVA films) to achieve stable solid-state fluorescence emission [13,14]. However, there are still some problems with this approach. For example, the concentration of CDs after doping is limited, and the base material not only increases the cost, but also may affect the properties of CDs.

In order to solve the above problems, aggregation-induced effect (AIE) CDs have attracted more and more researchers’ attention. Different from common water-soluble CDs, AIE CDs are a kind of hydrophobic CDs. Different from the conventional composite solid-state fluorescent CDs, the solid-state fluorescence emission of AIE CDs exhibits obvious differences compared to its solution state. At present, many studies on AIE have been reported. For example, Yang et al. synthesized H-CDs by acetic acid solvothermal method using dithiodibenzoic acid (DSTA) and melamine (MA) and applied them to anti-counterfeiting inks [15]. Arshad et al. synthesized CDs using para-Benzoquinone and l-cysteine. Interestingly, CDs can act as moisture sensors in the form of molecular switches to detect water content in various solvents [16]. The unique properties of AIE CDs enable it to have a wider range of application scenarios and show great development potential.

Light-emitting diode (LED) is a new generation of green lighting sources, with advantages including energy saving, environmental protection, long life and cold light emission, etc [17,18]. For now, LED is currently widely used in all aspects of life. So far, LED color conversion materials mainly include phosphorescent materials, semiconductor quantum dots and fluorescent materials [19,20]. Phosphorescent materials tend to aggregate at the micrometer scale, limiting the color conversion efficiency of LEDs. Semiconductor quantum dots contain heavy metals such as Cd, Cr, etc., which pollute the environment and human body seriously [21]. The calcination temperature of traditional fluorescent powders is mostly around 1000 °C, and rare earth elements need to be consumed. At present, CDs are environment friendly and have good biocompatibility due to its simple and green synthesis conditions. For example, Da et al. successfully synthesized multi-color fluorescent CDs with tunable emission wavelength by changing the reaction solvent using tartaric acid and triammonium citrate as prereactants and applied them to white light LEDs [22]. Ma et al. produced warm white light emitting LED by preparing phosphors from microwave-assisted sodium silicate pyrolysis and assembling them with PVA films [23]. However, most of the CDs are dispersed in solution, and ACQ makes CDs unable to emit light in solid form. In the meantime, the CDs have poor compatibility with the epoxy resin commonly used in LED packaging materials. On the other hand, most of the prepared LEDs emit warm white light, while the monochromatic long-wave LEDs with higher color purity are rarely reported. Therefore, AIE CDs with high quantum yield are expected to be a new generation of optical conversion materials for LEDs.

As mentioned above, high quantum yield and inhibition of solid-state luminescence quenching are key factors for CDs preparation of high-color purity LEDs. In this work, we used DSTA as the carbon source and MA, urea, sulfonamide as the nitrogen source. According to relevant reports, the existence of disulfide bonds (S–S) hinders the stacking degree of π-π bond, thence AIE CDs do not exhibit ACQ in solid-state [15,24]. The nitrogen-rich components of MA, urea and sulfa play a role in emission regulation. This is an attempt to change the emission characteristics of the CDs. The synthesized tri-color AIE CDs prove that our scheme is simple and feasible, which is of great significance for further promoting the light conversion application and synthetic regulation of CDs. Furthermore, Tri-color AIE CDs has been successfully used to produce monochromatic LED with higher color purity and CRI, as well as in fingerprint detection with effective details. Scheme 1 displayed the schematic diagram of preparation, LEDs and fingerprint detection.

Dithiobenzoic acid was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), Melamine, urea and sulfonamide were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China). Acetic acid was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China). Deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were performed using JEM-1200 EX (Japan, JEOL) transmission electron microscope. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was displayed with a Rigaku Ultima IV (Japan) X-ray diffractometer, and the measurement was performed with CuKα radiation in the 2θ range of 0°/5°–90°. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on a spectrometer (NICO-LET5700) by the KBr tablet method, with a scanning range of 400–4000 cm−1 and a resolution of 4 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on KRATOS AXIS Ultra DLD (Japan). Raman spectra were recorded with Thermofisher DXR2 using a laser wavelength of 785 nm. The Thermogravimetric Analysis (TG) of CDs was conducted using a NETZSCH TG209F3 instrument in nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. Fluorescence (FL) measurement was performed on a Fluorospectrophotometer (F-7000) (Hitachi, Japan), with a scanning range of 300–700 nm. UV–Vis spectroscopy was performed on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (T9) from 200 to 800 nm. The fluorescence decay curve was performed with Edinburgh FLS980. LED photoelectric performance was performed with HP9000 (HongPu, Hangzhou, China). Quantum yield (QY) was measured using the Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of R-CDs Y-CDs and G-CDs powers
ce:section.ce:para: 250 mg of DSTA and 100 mg of MA were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. The solution was transferred to 40 mL Teflon reactor and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. The reacted solution was filtered after centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The resulting solution was called CD1. Subsequently, CD1 was poured into 150 mL boiling water, then solid component was obtained after centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, dry the solid component to obtain R-CDs.
MA was replaced with the same amount of urea and sulfonamide, respectively. Then CD2 and Y-CDs, CD3 and G-CDs were obtained through the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of red, yellow and blue LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare LED devices, 10 mg R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs were weighed and fully mixed with 10 g epoxy resin, then coated on 365 nm GaN chip by drop coating and dried at 100 °C for 1h. The forward operating voltages (Vf) and reverse operating voltages (Vr) of GaN chip were 3.38V and 5.00V, respectively. The LED devices were photoelectric tested on HP9000 (HongPu, Hangzhou, China) instrument, with scan range of 380–780 nm. R-CDs, Y-CDs, G-CDs were considered as color conversion layers to obtain LEDs with different color emission. All measurements were performed under dark condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs was obtained through solvothermal reaction by adding different nitrogen sources (melamine, urea, sulfa) with DSTA as precursor and acetic acid as solvent. Acetic acid played an important role in the formation of CDs, which was the catalyst for CDs carbonization and surface constitution [15]. TEM, XRD and Raman spectroscopy were taken to confirm the structure properties of the as-prepared R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 A–C, TEM images showed the morphologies of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs were spherical. Besides, HR-TEM displayed that the lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the graphite (100) plane [25,26]. Meanwhile, the single crystal structures of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs were observed by FFT diffraction patterns, the lattice spacing was consistent with HR-TEM. Fig. 1D–F exhibited the specific size distribution of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs, concentrated between 2 and 7 nm. It was interesting that although the sizes of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs were concentrated between 2 and 7 nm, the average particle sizes of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs gradually decreased according to the Gaussian curve of particle size distribution, which may be account for their corresponding fluorescence emission. The statistical calculation results showed that the average particle size of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs was 4.35 nm, 4.12 nm and 3.51 nm, respectively. That is, the fluorescence emission blue shift due to the reduction of particle size [27]. XRD showed that R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs had an obvious carbon peak at 2θ = 25°, which was attributed to their graphitized carbon cores, while the weak peak near 41° represented the interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm. In addition, the peaks before 2θ = 20° in DSTA had almost disappeared, which proved the higher degree of carbonization in R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs (Fig. 1G), [15,28]. Additionally, the Raman spectra showed strong 2D absorption peaks for R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs, indicating that the CDs formed a graphene-like stacking structure. Meanwhile, each CDs showed weak D and G bands associated with disordered and graphitic structures (Fig. S1) [11].

XPS and FT-IR spectra were further performed to characterize the chemical structure of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs. The full XPS spectra of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs presented in Fig. 1H showed four peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.2 eV, 532.2 eV and 163.7 eV approximately, illustrating that C, N, O, S were included in three CDs. The atomic ratios of C, N, O, and S elements of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs were calculated and presented in Table S1. It can be seen that the nitrogen content of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs is 7.95%, 5.62% and 3.88%, respectively. With the increase of nitrogen content, the emission behavior of CDs was gradually red-shifted, which may be another factor affecting the optical properties [29]. Furthermore, in high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs, the four peaks centered about 284.6 eV, 285.1 eV, 286.6 eV and 286.9 eV confirmed the existence of C–C/CC, CO/CN, C–O and C–N, respectively (Figs. S2A–C) [24,30–32]. And the chemical bond content of high-resolution C 1s of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs were shown in Table S2. 531.5 eV and 533.0 eV of O 1s demonstrated that CO and C–O moieties existed in R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs (Figs. S3A–C) [31,33–35]. Table S3 displayed the proportion of the two peaks, respectively. The N 1s peaks at 399.2 eV and 399.9 eV indicated that nitrogen existed mostly in the form of pyridinic-N (N in 6-membered ring), pyrrolic-N (N in 5-membered ring) (Figs. S4A–C) [24,30,34]. The content ratio of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N was very different in Table S4. The pyridinic group on the heterocyclic surface determined the hydrophobicity of AIE CDs [15,36]. At the same time, the high content of pyridine group promoted the red shift of the fluorescence emission of CDs. The S 2p peak existed at around 163.3 eV, corresponding to S–C [16]. Moreover, S–H appeared at around 164.5 eV and S–S appeared at around 164.8 eV for R-CDs Y-CDs and G-CDs (Figs. S5A–C). [30]. The ratios of S–C, S–H, S–S can also be found in Table S5. It is worth noting that the proportion of S–S in the S2p spectrum decreased from 39.6% (R-CDs) to 10.7% (G-CDs), indicating that symmetric molecules associated with S–S, which was closely related to n-π* transitions of C–N/S absorption peaks in the UV spectrum [7]. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 1I, FT-IR demonstrated that O–H and N–H appeared at 3450 cm−1, CN/C–O appeared at 1400 cm−1, C–C appeared at 1520 cm−1, C–H appeared at 1390 cm−1, C–S appeared at 690 cm−1 and S–S appeared at 550 cm−1 [31,37,38]. In addition, DSTA showed wider absorption peaks at 3050 cm−1, which indicated that after amidation and carbonization of hydroxyl and amino groups in raw material, the reduction of the content of these hydrophilic groups increased the hydrophobicity of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs to a certain extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of R-CDs, Y-CDs, G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To comprehensively explore the optical properties of AIE CDs, FL spectra of CD1, CD2 and CD3 (liquid state) was first recorded. As shown in Fig. 2 A–C, the maximum excitation wavelengths for CD1, CD2 and CD3 were 400 nm, 410 nm and 390 nm, respectively. The corresponding optimal emission wavelengths appeared at 470 nm, 510 nm and 475 nm respectively, showing a typical blue luminescent for CD1 and CD3 and green luminescent for CD2 under UV lamp. Significantly, the emission behaviors of CD1, CD2 and CD3 were all located in the short-wave region. According to related reports, the emission behavior of carbon quantum dots with small particle size tended to the short-wave blue region, which provided a prerequisite for the aggregation-induced behavior of carbon dots [22,27].

Fig. 2D–F provided detailed emission spectra of CD1, CD2, and CD3 at different excitation wavelengths. Although the fluorescent color of CD1 and CD3 were very similar, their emission behaviors were slightly different. The emission of CD1 exhibited a typical unimodal emission with excitation-dependent behavior, whereas the emission of CD3 consisted of two distinct peaks. There is no unified explanation for the luminescence mechanism of CDs. The luminescence mechanism proposed at present includes quantum size effect [39,40], surface state [41], edge state[42,43] and eigenstate luminescence [44], molecular fluorophore [45–47] and so on. In edge and eigenstate luminescence, there are two emission centers: one originating from electron conjugated structures (eigenstate emission) and the other associated with surface energy traps (surface functional group emission). The shortwave emission is mainly from the eigenstates of the carbon core of CDs, while the longwave emission is mainly from CDs surface defects and functional groups [22,48]. At the same time, the double emission behavior is closely related to the presence of p-amino groups in the precursor reactants. The conjugated structure of the carbon source also plays a crucial role in the preparation of CDs with double fluorescence emission [49,50]. CD2 displayed a single green emission peak and exhibited excitation-independent behavior. In acetic acid solution, CD1, CD2 and CD3 formed well dispersion, so the emission in liquid state was concentrated in the shortwave region. Interestingly, CD2 synthesized with urea as a nitrogen source showed green emission, possibly because urea does not possess the carbon ring structure similar to MA and sulfa. The conjugate structure in the precursor can better regulate the band gap rather than the size effect. Therefore, the emission color in the product was more affected [51,52].

The preparation of AIE CDs was based on Yang's method [15]. CD1, CD2, CD3 were induced to aggregate respectively with 150 mL deionized water and purified to obtain solid-state CDs powders without adding other solid matrix, which were named R-CDs, Y-CDs, G-CDs. Further, we investigated the optical properties of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs. Fig. 3 A showed the maximum excitation, emission and solid-state UV–Vis spectra of R-CDs. It can be seen that after induction and purification, CD1 aggregated into a red solid powder, while avoiding aggregation quenching effect due to stacking. Meanwhile, the excitation and emission of R-CDs were red-shifted to 560 nm and 615 nm, respectively. In addition, the UV–Vis spectrum of R-CDs demonstrated two distinct absorption peaks at 300 nm, 455 nm and 550 nm, which pointed to π-π* transitions of CC in carbon cores, n-π* transitions of C–N/S and n-π* transitions of surface states with CO and CO/S structures, respectively [53,54]. Fig. 3B showed the emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of R-CDs. Interestingly, the excitation-dependent behavior of CD1 had disappeared after AIE, with the emission center stabilized at 615 nm. Furthermore, the CIE coordinates of R-CDs were (0.68, 0.31), proving that R-CDs displayed red emission (Fig. 3C).

UV–Vis spectrum showed that Y-CDs appeared similar absorption peaks with R-CDs at 310 nm, 455 nm and 550 nm (Fig. 3D) [53,55]. The maximum excitation and emission of Y-CDs were located at 470 nm and 510 nm, respectively. However, different from the emission spectra of R-CDs, the emission of Y-CDs exhibited a dual-emission behavior. The stronger emission center at 510 nm and the weaker emission center at 600 nm worked together to affect the fluorescent color of Y-CDs (Fig. 3E). In addition, the CIE coordinates of Y-CDs were (0.38, 0.60), which was close to the luminescence in the yellow region (Fig. 3F).

Interestingly, the fluorescence spectra of G-CDs were very similar to Y-CDs. The optimal excitation and emission of G-CDs were located at 470 nm and 510 nm, respectively. However, only the π-π* transitions of CC at 300 nm and the n-π* transitions of CO or C–N/S at 450 nm appeared in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 3G) [24,56]. Unlike Y-CDs, the second emission center at 600 nm was blue-shifted to a weaker shoulder at 570 nm, which resulted in a small change of fluorescence color (Fig. 3H). Therefore, the coordinates of G-CDs in the CIE were active in the green range, and the coordinates of the optimal emission were (0.25, 0.59) (Fig. 3I). Significantly, unlike most previously reported CDs, R-CDs, Y-CDs, and G-CDs showed almost no excitation dependence behavior, and their emission centers were all very stable. In addition, yellow and green emission could be obtained only by adjusting the emission intensity at about 600 nm in dual-emission. This was extremely rare in previous reports of multicolor regulation. In addition, Fig. S6 demonstrated that time-resolved fluorescence decays were determined and the obtained lifetime decay curves were fitted well by a double-exponential function y(t) with lifetimes ι1 and ι2 (eq. (1)) and the average fluorescence lifetimes (τave) was calculated according to eq. (2), [6].

ave of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs were 5.92 ns, 4.75 ns and 2.84 ns, respectively. Due to the spectral overlap between the emission of the graphitized nucleus and the absorption of the heteroatomic cluster-enriched surface, the energy absorbed by the nucleus can be transferred from the graphitized nucleus to the heteroatomic cluster-enriched surface. A shorter fluorescence lifetime, due to resonance energy transfer (RET) or direct π-π interactions, means that a more intense fluorescence resonance energy transfer has occurred [57]. Finally, the QY of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs were measured to be 16.04%, 2.87% and 3.79%, respectively. Due to the layered structure, high crystallinity and doping, the QY of R-CDs was significantly stronger than that of Y-CDs and G-CDs [58].

It is well known that CDs are susceptible to lose fluorescence in the absence of dispersing solvent due to the ACQ effect. This is mainly attributed to the stacking of π-π conjugation in the solid-state, while the aggregation of large conjugated systems consumes the transition energy (Fig. 4 A) [7]. Whereas, the prepared AIE CDs can still maintain fluorescence emission in the solid-state. On the one hand, the presence of S–S inhibits π-π conjugate stacking. The suppressed aggregation affects the fluorescence emission behavior. On the other hand, CDs assemble continuously due to hydrophobicity, and the carbon core is partially subjected to ACQ, leading to the shutdown of shortwave emission. At the same time, the intramolecular rotation (RIR) of the disulfide bond axis of axisymmetric heterocyclic compounds is limited, and the powders are grouped together to display long-wave emission fluorescence. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S7, under the fluorescence microscope, R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs in pure acetic acid exhibited blue, green and blue fluorescence respectively, and the particle size showed a smaller distribution. It is worth noting that R-CD also shows red fluorescence in acetic acid, which is due to low solubility, leading to partial aggregation. While in 100% water, R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs aggregated into larger particles and the fluorescence shifted to red, yellow and green respectively. This result fully proved the generation of aggregation behavior from the aspects of luminescence behavior and micro morphology. In addition, in a good solvent (acetic acid), the intense vibration of S–S consumes the energy of the excited state and leads to fluorescence quenching. With the addition of bad solvent (water), CDs begin to accumulate and intramolecular vibration is limited, at which time the energy is mainly dissipated by radiation [59]. This is slightly different from the traditional AIE which transforms from non-luminous dispersed state to luminous aggregate state [24]. We call this AIE conversion luminescence, that is, AIE behavior changes the emission behavior of CDs. In detail, R-CDs was dissolved in acetic acid stably, but not in water. As the water volume increases to 0/20/40/60/80/100%, the red fluorescence from the aggregated state and the blue fluorescence from the solution state compete with each other (Fig. S8A). In the water volume content of 100%, the CDs system is cloudy, and the precipitation is complete within 1 h, so as the other two CDs (Fig. S8B&C). This result further confirmed the AIE behavior of CDs by water. Additionally, the pyridinic N and pyridinic N in the structure of the prepared CDs belong to the conjugated nitrogen groups, which are rigid conjugated structures. The rigid conjugated structures lead to less nonradiative transitions, such as the heat release caused by single bond rotation. Hence, the radiative transitions increased and the brightness was improved [21]. Fig. 4B mainly showed how CD1 led to R-CDs’ fluorescence generation by inhibiting the rotation of intramolecular disulfide bonds. The formation mechanism of Y-CDs and G-CDs was consistent with this. Remarkably, our experimental results proved that the disulfide bond in DSTA was the main factor affecting AIE. Different nitrogen sources only played the role of emission regulation on the final synthesized CDs, while did not affect the original AIE. The rich emission color also opened up the possibility of assembling multicolor LED.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs in LEDs and fingerprint detection
ce:section.ce:para: The highly efficient solid state fluorescence will significantly expand the applications of CDs [30,31,36]. Based on the above results, the prepared AIE CDs were considered applying for LEDs conversion materials. In order to avoid the influence of the own color of LED, 365 nm commercial GaN substrate was chosen as the excitation light source to fabricate the LEDs. Fig. 5 A showed the electroluminescence (EL) spectrum of red LEDs (R-LED) fabricated from R-CDs. The emission center of R-LED was at 620 nm under 140 mA current intensity. With the increase of the current intensity, the wavelength of the luminescence center for R-LED did not change, proving that the luminescence of R-LED showed obvious stability (Fig. 5B). Meanwhile, the optimal color coordinates of R-LED were (0.70, 0.29) and all CIE coordinates were located in red zone under different current intensities (Fig. 5C). In addition, the color purity of R-LED reached an astonishing 98.8%, corresponding to a color temperature (Tc) of 1433 K under 140 mA current intensity. With the same method, Y-LED and G-LED were fabricated with Y-CDs and G-CDs, respectively. The EL spectra of Y-LED showed that its emission band had a main peak at 595 nm and a shoulder at 545 nm, which made Y-LED appear orange-yellow color (Fig. 5D). In the range of 20–120 mA current intensity, the luminescence intensity of Y-LED increased with the increase of current intensity, while the luminescence center was obviously stable (Fig. 5E). Furthermore, the CIE coordinates of Y-LED were located in orange-yellow region and the optimal emission coordinates were (0.48, 0.49) (Fig. 5F). More importantly, the color purity of Y-LED also reached 93.1%, the positions and ratios of the main peak and shoulder peak were calculated at 577 nm. Different from the narrow emission peaks of R-LED and Y-LED, the EL spectra of G-LED showed a broad emission peak. The emission center was located at 525 nm, showing a distinct green color (Fig. 5G). Likewise, the spectra of the G-LED only changed in intensity at 20–120 mA current range (Fig. 5H). The optimal CIE coordinate was (0.32, 0.51), which corresponded to the ideal green light (Fig. 5I). Unfortunately, the color purity of G-LED was only 57.4%, and the Tc was reaching 4936 K. This may be related to its wider emission band.

Due to the good luminescence effect of the prepared three kinds of LEDs, in order to better apply to the actual lighting, we also considered the influence of other parameters in their EL spectrum. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the Correlated Color Temperature (CCT) of both R-LED and Y-LED was at a low level. The best performing R-LED was only about 1433 K. G-LEDs exhibited higher CCT, which was considered that the green region had a narrow wavelength range in the spectrum and inevitably brought a relatively high CCT. At the same time, the CCT of R-LED, Y-LED and G-LED showed obvious stability with the change of current intensity. In addition, the maximum luminous efficiencies of R-LED, Y-LED, G-LED were 89.55 l m/w, 114.2 l m/w and 55.56 l m/w, respectively (Fig. 6B). The increase in current intensity brought about an increase in temperature, which reduced the luminous efficiency slightly. Finally, the color rendering indices (CRI) of R-LED, Y-LED, G-LED were also considered as one of the important indicators for practical applications [18]. Fig. 6C showed that the CRI of the three LEDs had reached the level of over 60%. R-LED possessed the highest CRI of 71.4%. The electroluminescence parameters of R-LED, Y-LED and G-LED showed that R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs can be used as optical conversion materials in LEDs, and the prepared LED had good luminescence effect, high color purity and high CRI. The LEDs fabrication process was exhibited in Fig. 6D.

In addition, multi-color emitting AIE CDs also provided a potential possibility for preparing white emitting LEDs. In this work, R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs were mixed together in different proportions for W-LED according to the previous method. It was worth noting that 480 nm blue GaN chip was used to supplement the missing blue light. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the electroluminescence spectrum showed that three emission peaks at 480 nm, 530 nm and 600 nm existed in W-LED, which constituted white emission. Fig. 7B showed that the emission peak position remained stable as the current intensity increased. In addition, the luminous efficiency of W-LED was slightly reduced as that of previous devices. However, the CRI had reached a high level of 80.7, which had met the strict application requirements like classroom lighting (Fig. 7C) [60]. Finally, the CCT of W-LED was stable around 5200 K under different current intensities and the CIE coordinates was (0.33, 0.38), showing obvious warm white light emission (Fig. 7D). AIE CDs provided an effective idea for the preparation of new LED and was expected to become one of the color conversion materials of new generation monochrome and warm white LED.

In addition to being used as a light-conversion material for LEDs, the ACQ resistance of AIE CDs makes it useful for fingerprint detection. Hence, we successfully detected latent fingerprints on the glass plate by taking advantage of the lipid-soluble luminescence properties of CDs [38]. Benefiting from the high contrast of fluorescent signals, the patterns of all samples can be clearly displayed. Clearly observed bifurcations, islands, terminations, eyes, nuclei, ridge divergence and intersection feature points (Fig. 8 A–C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, fluorescence intensity stability and thermal stability of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs were tested in order to meet the needs of daily use. Fig. S9A showed the stability change of R-CDs under different storage conditions. It can be seen that the relative fluorescence intensity basically remained unchanged after 0/1/2/3/4/5/6 h UV irradiation. Similarly, the relative fluorescence intensity remained stable after 0/5/10/15/20/25/30 days of sunlight exposure and 30/40/50/60/70/80 °C heating. Y-CDs and G-CDs also performed excellent fluorescence stability (Fig. S9B&C). Furthermore, because the operating temperature of LED device is up to 120 °C or so, the thermal stability of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs were still discussed. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was employed to investigate stability of R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs under nitrogen gas atmosphere. As shown in Fig. S10A, R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs can still maintain more than 90% of the quality below 250 °C. More importantly, R-CDs, Y-CDs and G-CDs had almost no heat loss at LED operating temperatures below 150 °C. As can be seen from the DTG curve, the sharp loss of mass occurs in the range of 300–350 °C, which may be ascribed to the dehydration and release of the surface functional groups on CDs. At this temperature range, some surface groups (such as carboxyl groups, side chains containing amino groups) began to decompose (Fig. S10B). Nevertheless, the high stability below 150 °C showed promise as a fluorescent conversion material for LED packaging which can be considered as an emerging LED luminescent material.

In summary, Three CDs that possesses short-wave emission luminescence in aqueous state and long-wave emission in solid state was successfully synthesized via a facial method. We call this AIE conversion luminescence, that is, AIE behavior changes the emission behavior of CDs. Further, through our experiments we proved that the disulfide bond played a major role in AIE. In the presence of water, CDs begin to self-assemble continuously due to hydrophobicity and the carbon core is partially subjected to ACQ, leading to the shutdown of shortwave emission. Meanwhile, intramolecular disulfide bond rotation is prohibited, and the energy conversion at the surface is converted into long-wave emission fluorescence. Additionally, the emission behavior of CDs was regulated by different nitrogen sources, so that AIE CDs with different colors could be obtained. Moreover, combing with the excellent thermal stability and photostability, we fabricated multi-color LEDs by applying the obtained solid-state AIE CDs. And the fabricated LEDs possess decent device performances, including good luminescence effect, high color purity (up to 98.8%), CRI (over 60%) and warm light low level CCT, indicating their potential for solid-state multicolor LED applications. In addition, various fine structures of fingerprints can be clearly seen in fingerprint detection. Thus, the AIE CDs prepared meet the application requirements of different aspects.
